mnemonics.title: MERGE Sorted Lists
mnemonics.content: MERGE: Move pointers, Extract smaller, Rearrange pointers, Go until one ends, End remaining list. Each step is O(1), total O(m+n).

mnemonics.title: Reverse Linked List Iterative
mnemonics.content: P-C-N Pattern: Prev = NULL initially, Current = head, iterate while Current exists: Save next, Reverse link, Move Prev and Current forward. End when Current = NULL, Prev is new head.

mnemonics.title: Floyd's Cycle Detection
mnemonics.content: Tortoise-Hare: Tortoise moves 1, Hare moves 2. If they meet, cycle exists. To find cycle start: reset one to head, move both 1 step, meet at cycle start. Proof: Distance from head to cycle start = distance hare catches tortoise.

acronyms.expansion: Doubly Linked List - nodes have prev and next pointers enabling bidirectional traversal and O(1) deletion
acronyms.usage: Used when frequent backward traversal or O(1) deletion given node pointer is required

acronyms.expansion: Circular Linked List - last node points to first, eliminating null-end checks in cyclic applications
acronyms.usage: Round-robin scheduling, circular buffers, problems requiring continuous cycling

acronyms.expansion: Singly Linked List - nodes contain data and next pointer only, unidirectional traversal
acronyms.usage: Basic linked structure, memory-efficient when backward traversal not needed

acronyms.expansion: First In First Out - queue behavior achievable with linked list using head for dequeue, tail for enqueue

visualTips.visualization: Visualize three cards in a row: prev (left, showing NULL), current (center), next (right). As you 'flip' current's arrow to point left, slide all three one position right. Think of redirecting arrows rather than reversing the list physically.

Two sorted decks of cards face-up. Compare top cards, place smaller in result pile, repeat. Visualize pointers as your fingers pointing to current cards being compared. When one deck empties, flip remaining deck entirely.

Imagine a grid with X marks for non-zeros. Draw horizontal chains connecting X's in each row (row-links) and vertical chains connecting X's in each column (col-links). Each X becomes a junction point in both a row-chain and column-chain.

analogies.concept: Linked list nodes as train cars
analogies.analogy: Each node is a train car with cargo (data) and a coupling hook (next pointer). Adding a car means unhooking, attaching new car, re-hooking. Reversing is like decoupling entire train and re-coupling in opposite direction car by car.
On a circular track, a faster runner (hare) will eventually lap a slower runner (tortoise). If the track is infinite (no cycle), the faster runner never returns. Meeting proves circular path exists.

analogies.concept: Polynomial addition as merging sorted sequences
analogies.analogy: Like combining two sorted vocabulary lists by reading both simultaneously: if words match (same exponent), combine; otherwise copy the next word from whichever list has the earlier word.
